topic: planning a trip to Isfahan with Shirin — her first time in Iran

turns.content: I am planning a trip to Isfahan with my wife and I could use some advice on logistics. She has never been to Iran and I want the experience to be perfect for her.
What a wonderful trip to plan! Isfahan is one of the most beautiful cities in the world. When are you thinking of going, and how long do you plan to stay?

We are looking at late March or early April — around Nowruz, which is the Persian New Year. The timing would make it especially meaningful. I am thinking ten days to two weeks. I want to give Isfahan the time it deserves rather than rushing through like a tourist checking boxes.

Nowruz timing is perfect for Isfahan. The weather in late March is mild — typically 15-20°C during the day — and the city comes alive with celebrations, gardens in bloom, and a festive atmosphere that you won't experience at any other time of year. Ten days to two weeks is ideal for really absorbing the city at a comfortable pace.

A few logistical questions: Will you be flying directly into Isfahan, or arriving via Tehran first? And are you planning to stay in one location or move around to other cities as well?

We would fly into Tehran first. I have family there that I would like to visit for two or three days before continuing to Isfahan. My cousin still lives in the Tajrish neighborhood where my family was originally from. From Tehran we would take the domestic flight to Isfahan, which I believe is approximately one hour.

Yes, the Tehran to Isfahan flight is about 55 minutes to an hour — Iran Air and Mahan Air both operate that route frequently. You could also take the train, which is roughly 6-7 hours but passes through beautiful scenery if you're up for a slower journey.

Starting with a few days in Tehran to visit family is a lovely way to ease into the trip, especially if your wife hasn't been before. Having family as local guides in Tehran will help her adjust to the pace and culture before you head to Isfahan.

For Isfahan itself, do you have specific sites you want to prioritize, or would you like me to help build an itinerary?

I would appreciate help with the itinerary, jan. I have a list in my head but I want to make sure I am not overlooking anything. The essentials for me are Naqsh-e Jahan Square, the Shah Mosque, the Armenian quarter in Julfa, and the Khaju Bridge at sunset. But Shirin is particularly interested in the arts — tile work, miniature painting, traditional crafts. I want to incorporate that dimension for her.

Your essentials are perfect — those are the anchors of any Isfahan visit. For Shirin's arts focus, here's what I'd add:

**Tilework & Architecture:**
- Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque — the tilework is arguably even more stunning than the Shah Mosque, and the way the light shifts through the dome is extraordinary
- The Friday Mosque (Jameh Mosque) — 800 years of architectural evolution in one building, every era's tile techniques side by side

**Traditional Crafts:**
- The bazaar workshops around Naqsh-e Jahan Square — many artisans still practice khatam-kari (inlaid woodwork), mina-kari (enamelwork), and ghalamzani (metalwork). You can watch them work and purchase directly.
- The School of Traditional Arts (Honar-haye Sonnati) — sometimes open for demonstrations

**Miniature Painting:**
- Visit the studios along Chahar Bagh Avenue. Several master miniaturists have open workshops where Shirin could see the painting process firsthand and even take a short lesson if she's interested.

Would you like me to organize these into a day-by-day structure?

Yes, please. And I should mention that Shirin will want time in the bazaars. She is a very deliberate shopper — she will spend an hour examining textiles, asking questions, comparing quality. I have learned over twenty-three years of marriage not to rush her in a market. So the itinerary should have generous time for that.

Ha — a wise approach after twenty-three years. Here's a rough day-by-day framework for seven days in Isfahan. I'll build in generous bazaar time:

**Day 1 — Arrival & Orientation**
Settle in, walk along the Zayandeh River in the evening, dinner nearby. Low-key start.

**Day 2 — Naqsh-e Jahan Square (Full Day)**
Morning: Shah Mosque, Ali Qapu Palace. Afternoon: The bazaar — give Shirin a full 3-4 hours here. The textile and carpet sections alone are worth extended time.

**Day 3 — Mosques & Bridges**
Morning: Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque (go early for the best light). Afternoon: Khaju Bridge and Si-o-se-pol. Return to Khaju at sunset.

**Day 4 — Armenian Julfa**
Vank Cathedral, the Armenian museum, and the quiet streets of the Julfa quarter. Lunch at a local Armenian restaurant. Afternoon: explore the galleries and shops in Julfa.

**Day 5 — Arts & Craft Day (Shirin's Day)**
Morning: Friday Mosque for the architectural timeline. Afternoon: Artisan workshops — khatam-kari, mina-kari, and miniature painting studios on Chahar Bagh. This is a full immersion day.

**Day 6 — Day Trip or Deeper Exploration**
Options: Drive to Abyaneh village (a red-clay mountain village about 2 hours away) or revisit favorite spots from earlier in the trip. This is your flex day.

**Day 7 — Final Bazaar Visit & Departure Prep**
Morning: Return to the bazaar for any purchases Shirin has been deliberating. Farewell walk through the gardens.

How does that feel as a skeleton?

That is excellent. I particularly appreciate that you gave Day Five to Shirin explicitly. She will love that. The Abyaneh village on Day Six is a wonderful suggestion — I had forgotten about it. The red clay architecture against the mountain backdrop would be stunning in spring. I think Shirin would find it very moving.

Abyaneh in spring is genuinely magical — the almond and cherry blossoms against the red clay is one of those scenes that stays with you. It's also a nice change of pace from the urban grandeur of Isfahan. The village women still wear traditional floral headscarves that are distinct to Abyaneh, and the whole place feels suspended in time.

I'd recommend hiring a local driver for the day trip rather than renting a car — the mountain roads are fine but navigating them yourself adds unnecessary stress. A driver can usually be arranged through your hotel for a very reasonable rate.

Are there any dietary considerations or hotel preferences I should factor in? Isfahan has excellent traditional courtyard hotels that would suit the atmosphere of the trip.

A traditional courtyard hotel is exactly what I want. I have heard of the Abbasi Hotel — it was originally a caravanserai, I believe. Three hundred years old. Shirin would be enchanted by a setting like that. We are not on a strict budget for this trip. I want it to be extraordinary for her.

The Abbasi Hotel is the premier choice in Isfahan and your instinct is spot on — it's a restored Safavid-era caravanserai dating to the early 1700s. The central courtyard with its gardens, fountains, and traditional architecture is absolutely stunning. Rooms vary in quality, so I'd recommend requesting a suite overlooking the inner courtyard rather than the street-facing rooms.

The hotel also has an excellent traditional restaurant where you can have dinner in the courtyard under the stars. For a special evening, it doesn't get much better than that setting.

Book well in advance if you're going during Nowruz — the Abbasi fills up quickly for the holiday season. Some travelers book three to four months ahead for that period.

It sounds like this trip is going to be deeply meaningful for both of you.

It will be, jan. I left Iran when I was very young and I have been back only twice as an adult. Showing Shirin where my family came from — not just Tehran but the broader culture, the art, the hospitality — is something I have wanted to do for years. Isfahan represents the best of what Persian civilization produced. I want her to see it with her own eyes. Thank you for helping me shape this. I will begin making reservations.

It sounds like a trip that's been a long time coming, and I think the way you're approaching it — with care, generosity, and real intention — will make it unforgettable for Shirin. Isfahan has a way of exceeding even high expectations.

Remember to book the Abbasi early, arrange that driver for the Abyaneh day trip through the hotel, and leave room in the itinerary for spontaneous discoveries. The best moments in Isfahan often happen when you're just wandering. Safe travels and enjoy every minute of it.
